Were You Asked This?
Design YouTube

Design a video streaming platform like YouTube where creators can upload and share videos, viewers can stream content on-demand, and users receive personalized recommendations based on their viewing history and preferences.

Asked at:

Datadog

Snapchat

O

other

Meta

+3 More
Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

YouTube is an on-demand video streaming platform where creators upload large video files and viewers watch them instantly across devices, with smooth playback, scrubbing, and shareable links.

Interviewers ask this to assess whether you can design for two distinct paths—creator (upload, processing, management) and viewer (low-latency playback at scale)—while handling large blobs, asynchronous workflows, and viral read traffic. They also probe your ability to manage cost, reliability, and abuse, and to make pragmatic trade-offs around storage, caching, and consistency. Expect to discuss uploads, transcoding pipelines (HLS/DASH), CDN strategy, metadata stores, and operational concerns like hotspots and backfills.

Hello Interview Problem Breakdown
YouTube

System design answer key for designing a video streaming platform like YouTube, built by FAANG managers and staff engineers.

View Answer Key
Community Solutions
Design YouTube
by Shashank Saurabh
0
1
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to upload large video files reliably, pause and resume interrupted uploads, and see progress.

Users should be able to watch videos on demand with smooth playback, adaptive quality, and the ability to seek.

Users should be able to share a link to a video and control visibility (public, unlisted, private).

Creators should be able to manage metadata (title, description, thumbnail), see processing status, and publish when ready.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you process uploaded videos to support adaptive bitrate streaming (HLS/DASH) and fast start times?

How do you support scalable, resumable uploads for very large files without overloading application servers?

How do you achieve low-latency global streaming and handle hotspots when a video goes viral?

How do you keep storage and compute costs under control as your catalog and traffic grow?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Handling Large Blobs

Video files are massive, making direct-to-object-storage uploads, multipart transfer, integrity checks, and CDN-backed delivery mandatory. Moving blobs through app servers would crush bandwidth, increase tail latency, and reduce reliability.

Read More
Managing Long Running Tasks

Transcoding, packaging, and thumbnail generation are CPU-intensive, multi-minute workflows that must be orchestrated asynchronously with retries, idempotency, and progress tracking to provide a robust creator experience.

Read More
Scaling Reads

View traffic dwarfs writes and is highly skewed when content goes viral. Caching manifests/segments at the CDN, hot metadata in-memory, and designing for hot-key mitigation are essential to survive spikes.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka decouples services and provides durable, high-throughput streams for events like upload-completed, transcode-started/completed, and playback telemetry. It enables reliable workflow triggers, backpressure handling, and auditing.

Read More
Redis

Redis offers sub-millisecond access for hot video metadata, signed playback tokens, rate limits, and sharded counters. It protects primary stores during viral spikes and supports hot-key mitigation strategies.

Read More
DynamoDB

DynamoDB is a globally available, horizontally scalable key-value store ideal for video metadata, upload session state, and access control records. Its adaptive capacity helps absorb uneven access patterns from trending content.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

YouTube

Identical architecture: large-file uploads, asynchronous transcoding to ABR (HLS/DASH), CDN distribution, and heavy read skew from viral videos with metadata-driven discovery.

Read
Practice
Dropbox

Shares reliable large-object uploads with resumability, integrity checks, and client-to-object-storage flows that bypass app servers, plus lifecycle management to control storage costs.

Read
Practice
Instagram

Focuses on media ingest and delivery at massive scale with aggressive CDN use, metadata-heavy reads, and background processing pipelines for media transformations and previews.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Serving video files directly from application servers without a CDN

This creates high latency, huge bandwidth costs, and a single bottleneck that collapses under viral load. A CDN with origin shielding and proper cache keys is essential for global performance and stability.

Synchronous transcoding during upload requests

Blocking user requests on CPU-heavy transcoding leads to timeouts, poor UX, and no recovery path for partial failures. Transcoding must be an asynchronous, durable workflow with retries and progress tracking.

Storing video blobs in the primary database

Databases are not optimized for large binary payloads, inflating storage costs and degrading query performance. Blobs belong in object storage with pre-signed URL access; keep the DB for metadata and access control.
